reflection: The initial reward function for `franka_pick` succeeded on iteration 0 with a perfect 1. 000 success rate and no evaluation errors, meaning the agent reliably solves the pick task as specified. What worked: the reward signal was well-shaped enough out of the gate to drive the policy to convergence without any compile/runtime issues. Nothing meaningful failed - there's no error to debug and no performance gap to close. Since the task is already saturated at 100%, further reward iteration on success rate alone has nothing to optimize.

Iteration 1 of `franka_pick` hit a perfect 1. 000 success rate on the first try, with no reward-shaping errors and no evaluation failures — the reward function compiled, ran, and drove the policy to solve the task end-to-end. Since this is also the running best, the current reward formulation is already at the ceiling for this benchmark and there is no failure mode to diagnose. What worked: the reward signal was dense and well-aligned enough that the policy converged without any tuning passes. What did not: nothing measurable — there is no shortfall to attribute to reward design.

Iteration 2 of `franka_pick` converged to a perfect 1. 000 success rate with a clean reward (no compile/runtime errors) and no evaluation faults, matching the best-so-far score. The reward shaping clearly produced a learnable gradient end-to-end — approach, grasp, and lift were all consistently rewarded enough for the policy to solve every rollout. Nothing measurable failed this iteration, so there is no defect to diagnose. Since the task is already saturated at ceiling, further reward edits risk regressing a working solution; the next "attempt" should either lock in this reward as the final accepted version, or move to a harder evaluation (more object variants, perturbed initial poses, stricter success criteria) to expose remaining weaknesses before tuning again.

Iteration 3 fully solved `franka_pick` with a 100% success rate and no evaluation errors, matching the best result observed so far. The reward function is working as intended — the shaping is dense enough to guide grasping and lifting, and the success criterion is being reliably triggered. Nothing failed in this run, so there is no error signal or regression to diagnose. Since the task is saturated at 1. 000, the next "attempt" should not chase higher success — instead, either (a) **lock in this reward and stop iterating** to avoid overfitting to a single seed, or (b) **stress-test robustness** by re-evaluating with more seeds, randomized object poses, or perturbed initial conditions before declaring convergence.

Iteration 4 of `franka_pick` achieved a perfect 1. 000 success rate with no evaluation errors, matching the best result so far and indicating the reward function is fully solving the task as defined. What worked: the current reward shaping converged the policy to reliably pick the object across all eval rollouts, with `reward status: ok` confirming no numerical instability or component failures. What did not: nothing measurable failed in this iteration — there is no residual signal to chase on the current metric. Since success rate has saturated, the next attempt should **not** alter the reward to push success higher (it is already at ceiling and further tweaks risk regression).
